title: The Sandbox Sequence and the Fourth Wall
display_title: The Sandbox Sequence and the Fourth Wall
wikitext:
[]: The Sandbox Sequence and the Fourth Wall             Quote Post by ran1 » Mon Aug 27, 2012 3:15 pm While I am working on a Formalist analysis of Episode 21 and EoE, the following here is a sort of off-topic divergence that I figured I'd post seperately, for your enjoyment. You can read EvaWiki's analysis of the sandbox scene here Joesph Balsam, in Jean Luc Godard's Week-End wrote: What a terrible film we're in...

[]:             Quote Post by MugwumpHasNoLiver » Mon Aug 27, 2012 6:45 pm Once again, your technical insight into film is sublimely fascinating. I can't really offer you much more than mere accolades on that part of your analysis, as I am far too out of my depth. I do think reading the dolls as mere stand-ins for Asuka and Rei may be a bit reductive. It may be true on some level, but we should take into account how they're depicted as grotesque. The pudgy doll with the black hair: notice her chubby cheeks, flattened eyes and what appears to be beard stubble on the underside of her chin. She can almost be seen as a caricature of an otaku shut-in. Now it's very likely not meant to directly represent beard stubble, as the dolls seem to drawn in a different art style from Shinji, using scribbled lines to represent shadow or texture. (Note that the same lines appear on the nose of the the pale, fair-haired doll.) You must admit though, the resemblance is uncanny. You can read this as more deliberate distancing on Anno's part by intentionally drawing attention to the unreal nature of the dolls, or you can read it as more commentary on otaku. Are these bizarre, semi-hermaphroditic creatures the end result of slipping into a solipsistic world that warps self-image? Is the fact that Shinji seems to be comfortable around them somewhat telling? As Shinji is completely self-concious and aware of his own fictional nature in this scene, the dolls might not only be representative of Rei and Asuka, but amalgamated succubi incorporating aspects of Shinji's self-loathing meant to further raise a mirror to the viewer. One question remains: Why did Gainax never make those two monstrosities into little bathing suit-clad figurines?

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Mon Aug 27, 2012 6:51 pm If the point was to "raise a mirror to the viewer" then it was a spectacular failure, because nothing about this scene made me look inward at all.

[]:             Quote Post by Blue Monday » Mon Aug 27, 2012 7:30 pm I love this scene, and on so many levels too. The points raised in the existing EvaWiki article, the fact it can serve as a microcosm to the movie itself and now this... Really fascinating write-up which is coming from a technical film aspect, of which I know next to nothing about, haha. I'm floored with just how much work must've gone into the scene (And the rest of the movie, for that matter), i.e. the grain effect visuals as a result of 'imitating' a high-frame rate camera - That's just genius.

[]:             Quote Post by Azathoth » Tue Aug 28, 2012 3:05 am I think you're very right to point out that what we're watching here is "Shinji Makes A Movie". Obviously the whole series is about Shinji's increasingly desperate attempts to chop the universe down until it fits into a narrative overlay that doesn't make his butt hurt so badly, but here yeah, Anno drops the "real" world and allows us to perceive the delusion outright. Worth noting, I think, that the scene is in some sense the hinge on which Shinji turns from the autism-comfort sad-sack he's heretofore been playing - segueing straight out of "this Rei is from your heart" - and afterwards segueing straight into the Asuka-catalyzed psychosexual implosion which I guess could be called the second act. As the last shot there hangs on Shinji's fucked-up attempt at artsy film-school shit, Asuka, his audience, loudly vents her rage at his incompetence and cowardice, precisely because she has already realized that she is guilty of the same crimes. This is probably the scene to which Shinji is specifically referring when he later mentions to Rei that he thought it would be alright to run away, but everything was still bad once he did - that is to say it is the point at which he begins to confront the vapidity/futility of his hero fantasy. More specifically, it's here that he admits the fantasy has failed to accomplish anything, and it's here that Asuka begins to make her last, devastatingly effective attack on him, forcing him to flee back into it despite its obvious unworkability. Given the significance of film grain here, I wonder what we might take from its presence in the Misato-Kaji sex scene that follows shortly after this. Perhaps you could say this is Misato's film instead, her attempt to lead by example, or to show him that he doesn't necessarily need to be ashamed of his weaknesses as a human being? Of course, Asuka hijacks this too with her viciously accurate assessment of this second film-within-a-film, and wrests the focus of the sequence back to herself with the utterly vicious "I wonder if I'll do things like Misato when I grow up" — what a statement to make fifteen minutes after your death! — and finally forces Shinji to act instead of hang back and watch, sit in a sandbox and film.

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Tue Aug 28, 2012 4:09 am ran1 wrote: We've arrived at the fundamental problem here, with Shinji, with his treatment of others -- they're dolls. They exist purely to encourage him, as in the "let's build a castle" line, and when they don't, when they have to go home (to their mother obviously, because Shinji doesn't have a mother, even in this fictionalized version of himself) they have to leave.

[]:             Quote Post by Azathoth » Tue Aug 28, 2012 10:40 am Monk Ed wrote: Shinji is not treating Rei and Asuka like they're dolls. He's treating dolls like they're Rei and Asuka.

[]:             Quote Post by Der Kommissar » Tue Aug 28, 2012 10:54 am To me, the grotesque appearance of the other two children was meant to communicate what a distorted and simplistic understanding of his closest two companions he has. Asuka, at least, will be alluding to as much shortly.

[]:             Quote Post by Oral Stage » Wed Aug 29, 2012 7:05 pm Her skin tone alone makes me think that the non-Asuka doll is supposed to be Nadia

[]:             Quote Post by Chroma » Tue Sep 11, 2012 10:05 pm What a fantastically interesting interpretation of this scene (a scene I could never get a firm grasp on myself). For what it's worth, this fourth-wall-breaking film within a film can still work with the many other interpretations and theories on this scene; the Freudian interpretation, for example, can still be just as valid as this, and they could even work together. Also, I totally agree with the doll analysis: Shinji doesn't see people as people, he sees them as things in his world that both are made to cheer him on and serve as the obstacles to blame his own shortcomings on. Bravo.

[]:             Quote Post by ran1 » Wed Sep 12, 2012 1:43 pm Well, I haven't been keeping up with my own analysis. That's either speaking to my considerably lessened ego or my considerably fuller schedule. In any event, here goes: Der Kommissar wrote: To me, the grotesque appearance of the other two children was meant to communicate what a distorted and simplistic understanding of his closest two companions he has. Asuka, at least, will be alluding to as much shortly.
